content.problemStatement:
**This is the easy version of the problem. Here, you need to print min‚Ā°(R,3)\min(R, 3)min(R,3) instead of RRR itself.**

You are given an integer NNN.

An array AAA is called good if it satisfies all of the following conditions:


1‚Č§Ai‚Č§N1 \le A_i \le N1‚Č§Ai‚Äč‚Č§N for all 1‚Č§i‚Č§‚ą£A‚ą£1 \le i \le |A|1‚Č§i‚Č§‚ą£A‚ą£
Ai‚Č§Ai+1A_i \le A_{i + 1}Ai‚Äč‚Č§Ai+1‚Äč for all 1‚Č§i<‚ą£A‚ą£1 \le i < |A|1‚Č§i<‚ą£A‚ą£
A1+A2+‚Ä¶+A‚ą£A‚ą£=NA_1 + A_2 + \ldots + A_{|A|} = NA1‚Äč+A2‚Äč+‚Ä¶+A‚ą£A‚ą£‚Äč=N
A1√óA2√ó‚Ä¶√óA‚ą£A‚ą£=NA_1 \times A_2 \times \ldots \times A_{|A|} = NA1‚Äč√óA2‚Äč√ó‚Ä¶√óA‚ą£A‚ą£‚Äč=N
In other words, the array should consist of non-decreasing positive integers and its product and sum both must be NNN.

Here, ‚ą£A‚ą£|A|‚ą£A‚ą£ denotes the length of AAA.

Let RRR denote the number of good arrays. Your task is to find the value min‚Ā°(R,3)\min(R, 3)min(R,3), i.e. you should be able to determine whether R=0,1,2R = 0, 1, 2R=0,1,2 or ‚Č•3\ge 3‚Č•3.

For example, for N=6N = 6N=6, R=2R = 2R=2 because there are 222 good arrays, [6][6][6] and [1,2,3][1, 2, 3][1,2,3].


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, containing a single integer NNN.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the value min‚Ā°(R,3)\min(R, 3)min(R,3).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: There is only one array A=[1]A = [1]A=[1].
Test case 444: There are 222 arrays: A=[4]A = [4]A=[4] and A=[2,2]A = [2, 2]A=[2,2].
Test case 111111: R=1043R = 1043R=1043, but we print min‚Ā°(R,3)=3\min(R, 3) = 3min(R,3)=3.
